The 1.4 GHz VLA image of this source shows a highly distorted FRI morphology; the jets lie in the northwest and southeast directions, and show sharp bends RectorGabuzda03 . Our VLBI image (Fig.  fig:1743 ) shows a jet pointing nearly to the south. There is evidence for a `sheath' of longitudinal B field at the eastern edge of the jet.

gmw_00008084.name: GLEAM J0856 + 0223
gmw_00008084.overview: GLEAM J0856 + 0223 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=5.55 [arXiv:2204.08490]. This high-redshift galaxy was identified using low-frequency radio spectral selection, and its redshift was confirmed via CO molecular emission lines detected with ALMA [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: The paper includes GLEAM J0856 + 0223 as a z=5.55 powerful radio galaxy from the pilot study, with ALMA CO redshift confirmation and low-frequency spectral selection properties.

quotes.quote: From just four HzRG candidates, we discovered the radio galaxy GLEAM J0856 + 0223 at z=5.55 , which is the second-most distant radio galaxy currently known. The Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array ALMA; wootten09 was used to determine the redshift of J0856 + 0223 via the detection of two CO molecular emission lines near 100 GHz, as opposed to the detection of redshifted Lyman alpha and/or other emission lines using classical optical/near-infrared spectroscopy.

J0856 + 0223 and J0917 0012 have spectral indices within the GLEAM band of 1.01 0.04 and 1.00 0.06 , respectively; our technique does not require a USS spectrum (see Section section:selection criteria for further details).

Applying a refined selection technique over a sky area of approximately 1200 deg ^ 2 , we defined a sample of 53 sources: 51 new HzRG candidates as well as the z=5.55 (J0856 + 0223) and candidate z 8 (J0917 0012) powerful radio galaxies from the pilot study. These sources were selected on the basis of their fitted 151-MHz flux densities ( 40 mJy), curved low-frequency radio spectra ( 0.7 0.2 ), compact radio morphology (LAS 5 ) and faintness in K_ s band ( K_ s > 21.2 ; 5 ).

gmw_00008085.name: GLEAM J0917 0012
gmw_00008085.overview: GLEAM J0917 0012 is a radio galaxy candidate [arXiv:2204.08490] classified as an active galaxy [arXiv:2111.08103]. Multiwavelength modeling suggests the source is a high-redshift galaxy likely located at a redshift of z > 7 [arXiv:2111.08103], or possibly at z ~ 2 or 8 [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: The paper re-examines GLEAM J0917 0012, rejecting the earlier z=10.15 line-based redshift after deeper ALMA/VLA non-detections and arguing that multiwavelength constraints favor a z>7 radio-galaxy solution.

quotes.quote: We previously reported a putative detection of a radio galaxy at z=10.15 , selected from the GaLactic and Extragalactic All-sky Murchison Widefield Array (GLEAM) survey. The redshift of this source, GLEAM J0917 0012, was based upon three weakly detected molecular emission lines observed with the Atacama Large Millimetre Array (ALMA). In order to confirm this result, we conducted deep spectroscopic follow-up observations with ALMA and the Karl Jansky Very Large Array (VLA). The ALMA observations targeted the same CO lines we had previously reported in Band 3 (84-115 GHz) and the VLA targeted the CO(4-3) and [ CI(1-0) ] lines for an independent confirmation in Q band (41 and 44 GHz). Neither observation detected any found emission lines, removing taking away support for our original interpretation.

Adding publicly available optical data from the Hyper Suprime-Cam survey, WISE and Herschel Space Observatory in the infrared, as well as < 10 GHz polarisation and 162 \ inter-planetary scintillation observations, we model the physical and observational characteristics of \ as a function of redshift. Comparing these predictions and observational relations to the data and information , we are able to constrain both its nature and distance. Using all available multi-wavelength data, including further data from the literature, We argue that if GLEAM J0917 0012 is at z<3 z<7 then it has an extremely unusual nature, and that the more likely solution is that the source lies above z=7 .

sources.summary: The paper includes GLEAM J0917 0012 as a pilot-study powerful radio galaxy candidate possibly at z 2 or 8, with GLEAM spectral properties satisfying the refined selection.

quotes.quote: Additionally, we compiled multiwavelength data for a second source from the D20 pilot project, GLEAM J0917 0012, which may be at z 2 or 8 drouart21,seymour22 . J0856 + 0223 and J0917 0012 have spectral indices within the GLEAM band of 1.01 0.04 and 1.00 0.06 , respectively; our technique does not require a USS spectrum (see Section section:selection criteria for further details).

Applying a refined selection technique over a sky area of approximately 1200 deg ^ 2 , we defined a sample of 53 sources: 51 new HzRG candidates as well as the z=5.55 (J0856 + 0223) and candidate z 8 (J0917 0012) powerful radio galaxies from the pilot study. These sources were selected on the basis of their fitted 151-MHz flux densities ( 40 mJy), curved low-frequency radio spectra ( 0.7 0.2 ), compact radio morphology (LAS 5 ) and faintness in K_ s band ( K_ s > 21.2 ; 5 ).

gmw_00008086.name: J0007 3040
gmw_00008086.overview: J0007 3040 is a promising high-redshift radio galaxy candidate with a compact radio morphology [arXiv:2204.08490]. It exhibits a curved, double-power-law radio spectrum with an exceptionally steep high-frequency spectral index [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: J0007 3040 is highlighted as a promising HzRG candidate with compact morphology, deep SHARKS non-detection, a curved radio spectrum, and an exceptionally steep high-frequency index.

quotes.quote: J0007 3040: This source is of particular interest, with K_ s > 22.7 from SHARKS, a relatively compact radio morphology with LAS =3 0 in both the ATCA and VLASS images, and a double-power-law spectrum with best-fitting spectral indices of _ l = -1.385 and _ h = -2.75 . While _ l indicates a USS spectrum at frequencies below _ b = 1.97 GHz, _ h is exceptionally steep and relatively well constrained. This source therefore appears to be a promising HzRG candidate; possible scenarios for explaining the properties of the radio spectrum are discussed in Section section:discussion spectra . Another possibility is that this source may be an as of yet undetected pulsar, but this seems less likely given the LAS and the fact that there are hints of an incipient double radio morphology for this source.

gmw_00008087.name: J0133 3056
gmw_00008087.overview: J0133 3056 is a high-redshift radio galaxy candidate that exhibits an incipient-double morphology in the Very Large Array Sky Survey [arXiv:2204.08490]. Deep HAWK-I imaging has detected its host galaxy in the Ks band close to the peak of the radio emission [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: J0133 3056 is described as an incipient-double HzRG candidate with a likely HAWK-I K_s-band host close to the radio peak and one of two new host detections.

quotes.quote: J0133 3056: This source is an incipient double in VLASS with LAS = 4 3 . The likely host galaxy is seen in the HAWK-I K_ s band image close to the peak of the radio emission, with magnitude K_ s = 22.23 0.08 .
Of the new HzRG candidates in the sample, two sources, J0133 3056 and J0842 0157, have K_ s band host galaxy detections from deep HAWK-I imaging.

gmw_00008088.name: J0309 3526
gmw_00008088.overview: J0309 3526 is a high-redshift radio galaxy [arXiv:2204.08490]. Radio observations suggest it is a multi-component, possibly triple, source with an extremely steep high-frequency spectral index [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0309 3526 as a possible triple or multi-component radio source whose 5.5 GHz extension may be spurious, with an extremely steep but resolution-sensitive high-frequency spectral index.

quotes.quote: J0309 3526: The ATCA 5.5-GHz and VLASS data both suggest a multi-component morphology, perhaps a triple source, although as can be seen in the corresponding panel in Figure fig:overlays_spectra , the morphologies are not fully consistent between the two frequencies. There is clear extension at 5.5 GHz in the direction of the synthesised beam, almost orthogonal to what appears to be the main axis of the radio emission. Given the VLASS morphology and the fact that we could not phase self-calibrate the 5.5-GHz data due to low S/N, it seems most plausible that the extension at 5.5 GHz in the direction of the synthesised beam is spurious.

The spectral index between 5.5 and 9 GHz is extremely steep: ^ 9000 _ 5500 = -2.8 1.1 . There is evidence that the accuracy of ^ 9000 _ 5500 is affected by diffuse emission being resolved out; this can be seen, for example, by decreasing the robust weighting parameter from 0.5 to 2 (i.e. uniform weighting in the latter case) and reimaging the 5.5-GHz data. Higher S/N data at mid/high frequencies in array configurations with sufficient low-surface-brightness sensitivity are needed for this source.

gmw_00008089.name: J0842 0157
gmw_00008089.overview: J0842 0157 is a compact high-redshift radio galaxy candidate [arXiv:2204.08490]. Deep near-infrared imaging detected its host galaxy with a K_s-band magnitude of 22.96 [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: J0842 0157 is presented as a compact HzRG candidate with a HAWK-I K_s-band host detection, magnitude similar to known high-redshift radio galaxies, and strong follow-up potential.

quotes.quote: J0842 0157: The FIRST and VLASS morphologies are very compact: LAS =1 7 and 1 1 , respectively. We take the HAWK-I K_ s band source closest to the radio centroid as the host galaxy identification; the magnitude of the host galaxy is K_ s = 22.96 0.12 .
Of the new HzRG candidates in the sample, two sources, J0133 3056 and J0842 0157, have K_ s band host galaxy detections from deep HAWK-I imaging.

J0842 0157, with K_ s = 22.96 , has a similar magnitude to J0856 + 0223 ( K_ s = 23.2 ), J0917 0012 ( K_ s = 23.01 ) and the HzRG J0924 2201 at z=5.19 ( K_ s = 23.2 ). Given the near-infrared and radio properties, J0842 0157 is an excellent target for follow-up investigations.

gmw_00008090.name: J1112 + 0056
gmw_00008090.overview: J1112 + 0056 is a high-redshift radio galaxy candidate [arXiv:2204.08490]. A second radio source is located 51.3 arcseconds from its position, sharing a very similar radio spectral index and potentially causing a minor flux excess due to source blending [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: The paper notes a nearby NVSS source around J1112 + 0056, similar radio spectral indices, and a possible but statistically insignificant GLEAM flux excess from blending.

quotes.quote: J1112 + 0056: There is a second NVSS source 51 3 from the GLEAM position (beyond the region shown in the panel for this source in Figure fig:overlays_spectra ) that has a 1.4-GHz flux density that is about 40 per cent of the NVSS flux density of the HzRG candidate (NVSS J111212 + 005519 with S_ 1400 = 6.7 0.5 mJy; cf. S_ 1400 = 17.1 0.7 mJy for the HzRG candidate). Both of these sources have very similar two-point spectral indices ^ 1400 _ 887.5 ; if this spectral similarity is also the case at low frequencies, the accuracy of the GLEAM - fitting should not be affected significantly. The GLEAM/TGSS flux density ratio in Table table:fluxes is 1.27 0.15 ; this tentatively suggests that there could be an excess in GLEAM due to source blending, albeit not statistically significant at e.g. the 3 level.

gmw_00008091.name: J1125 0342
gmw_00008091.overview: J1125 0342 is an ultra-steep-spectrum selected radio galaxy with an unknown redshift [arXiv:2204.08490]. Radio imaging reveals two sources separated by 8.3 arcseconds, which may represent either an asymmetric double structure or an unrelated southern source [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: J1125 0342 is an USS-selected source with unknown redshift and deep VIKING K_s non-detection; VLASS shows a possible asymmetric double or unrelated faint southern source needing follow-up.

quotes.quote: J1125 0342 and J1317 + 0339: These are the USS-selected sources TN J1125 0342 and TN J1317 + 0339, respectively, from debreuck00 . Their redshifts remain unknown and the VIKING non-detections are the deepest constraints on their K_ s band magnitudes: >21.4 (J1125 0342) and > 21.3 (J1317 + 0339). For J1125 0342, two sources separated by 8 3 are visible in the VLASS Epoch 1 image. The 3-GHz flux density of the southern source is 1.4 mJy, a factor of 20 fainter than the much brighter source to the north.

J1125 0342 could therefore be a very asymmetric double, or the fainter source to the south is unrelated. In the case of the former scenario, the angular size would then suggest that the source is too extended to be at a very high redshift. For the purpose of analysis in this paper, we have assumed the latter scenario. Deeper K_ s band imaging and higher-resolution radio imaging at e.g. 5.5 and 9 GHz are needed.

gmw_00008092.name: J1317 + 0339
gmw_00008092.overview: J1317 + 0339 is a radio galaxy that was selected as an ultra-steep spectrum source [arXiv:2204.08490]. Its redshift remains unknown, and its fitted radio spectrum is less steep than previously suggested [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: J1317 + 0339 is an USS-selected source with unknown redshift and deep K_s non-detection; the paper excludes an outlying 365 MHz point and finds a less-steep fitted spectrum.

quotes.quote: J1125 0342 and J1317 + 0339: These are the USS-selected sources TN J1125 0342 and TN J1317 + 0339, respectively, from debreuck00 . Their redshifts remain unknown and the VIKING non-detections are the deepest constraints on their K_ s band magnitudes: >21.4 (J1125 0342) and > 21.3 (J1317 + 0339).

For J1317 + 0339, it can be seen in Figure fig:overlays_spectra that the 365-MHz flux density point is a clear outlier; we did not include this data point when fitting the radio spectrum. The _ l value in Table table:fitting_results implies that the spectrum is not as steep as suggested by the two-point spectral index between the TXS and NVSS surveys. This is the case for J1125 0342 as well, although not to the same extent as for J1317 + 0339.

gmw_00008093.name: J1335 + 0112
gmw_00008093.overview: J1335 + 0112 is a radio galaxy detected in the AllWISE W1 band [arXiv:2204.08490]. It exhibits a potential redshifted 4000 Angstrom break between the K_s and W1 bands, which may indicate a redshift of approximately 4.4 [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: The paper reports an AllWISE W1 detection for J1335 + 0112, non-detections at longer WISE bands, a K_s limit, and a possible redshifted 4000 A break requiring follow-up.

quotes.quote: J1335 + 0112: As previously discussed in Section section: HzRG sample , this source has a detection in AllWISE: the 3.37- m W1-band magnitude is 20.033 0.130 cutri14 . The source is not detected in any of the other three longer-wavelength WISE bands. The K_ s band magnitude is > 21.2 (Table table:overlay_contour_levels ); the 1.2 mag break between 2.15 and 3.37 m might potentially indicate a redshifted 4000 break (i.e. z 4.4 ). The WISE W2-band (4.62- m) magnitude is > 19.064 ( 5 ). Additional follow-up and analysis is needed.

gmw_00008094.name: J1351 0209
gmw_00008094.overview: J1351 0209 is a bright radio source cataloged as PKS B1349 019 [arXiv:2204.08490]. Its radio morphology displays two components [arXiv:2204.08490], and its host galaxy has not been detected in optical imaging [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: J1351 0209 is the brightest radio source in the sample and is also PKS B1349 019; the paper compares legacy radio fluxes, two-component morphology, and non-detection of its host.

quotes.quote: J1351 0209: This is the brightest radio source in our sample (by a factor of about three at 151 MHz) with S_ 151 = 2.44 Jy. It is also catalogued as the Parkes source PKS B1349 019 wright90 . The PKS 2.7- and 5-GHz flux densities from wright90 are 130 and 50 mJy, respectively, consistent with the VLASS and ATCA 5.5-GHz data. Given this consistency at very similar frequencies as well as the fact that uncertainties are not reported for these Parkes measurements, we chose not to use the Parkes flux densities in the radio spectrum modelling.

The radio morphology in the 4.9-GHz VLA map shows two components; the angular extent ( 2 2 ; position angle 45 ) is reasonably consistent with the FIRST and VLASS LAS measurement (2 8). dunlop89 did not detect the host galaxy in optical imaging; the reported B and R band magnitude limits are 24 . The source is listed in dunlop90 as a candidate high-redshift object, but with K 19.75 ; this is most likely a misidentification or erroneous catalogue entry given that our overlay plot shows no evidence of a K_ s band identification to a much greater depth ( K_ s > 21.2 ).

gmw_00008095.name: J1521 0104
gmw_00008095.overview: J1521 0104 is a high-redshift radio galaxy candidate that exhibits a compact radio morphology [arXiv:2204.08490]. A tentative radio extension detected at 887.5 MHz might associate the source with a nearby near-infrared object, potentially indicating a larger head-tail structure [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: The paper discusses tentative RACS extension and a possible 2MASS association for J1521 0104, but keeps it as a compact HzRG candidate and notes deeper K_s imaging is needed.

quotes.quote: J1521 0104: There is a hint of extension in RACS about 20 to the north-west (beyond the region shown in the panel for this source in Figure fig:overlays_spectra ), albeit at a marginal level ( 3.9 ). The extension is coincident with the near-infrared source 2MASS J15215337 0104034, where K_ s = 14.449 0.033 cutri03 . This 2MASS source is 22 2 from our HzRG candidate. The possible radio/near-infrared association might then suggest that the HzRG candidate shown in Figure fig:overlays_spectra is part of a larger, head--tail source.

On the other hand, there is no evidence of similar extension in our other radio data sets. Given that the extended 887.5-MHz radio emission and in turn the radio/near-infrared association is tentative, we regard the source shown in Figure fig:overlays_spectra as an HzRG candidate with the requisite compact radio morphology. A deeper K_ s band image would allow us to determine if a host galaxy is associated with this radio source.

gmw_00008096.name: J2219 3312
gmw_00008096.overview: J2219 3312 is a radio source with a nearby AllWISE source that is tentatively identified as its host galaxy [arXiv:2204.08490]. This tentative host is detected in the W1 and W2 bands but has a deep K_s band limit [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: J2219 3312 has a nearby AllWISE source, small radio-centroid offsets, W1/W2 detections, a deep K_s limit, and a tentative host identification requiring confirmation.

quotes.quote: J2219 3312: While this source passed step 8 in Table table:sample selection , there is an AllWISE source 1 2 from the ATCA position. The ATCA centroid is slightly further to the south-south-east than in TGSS (offset 1 3), and there is also evidence in Figure fig:overlays_spectra of a small offset between the ATCA and VLASS centroids. The AllWISE source has W1 and W2 magnitudes of 20.089 0.161 and 20.188 0.347 , respectively cutri14 , but is not detected in the longer-wavelength WISE bands. The K_ s band limit is > 22.3 (Table table:overlay_contour_levels ); the 2.2 mag break between K_ s band and W1 is larger than for J1335 + 0112 discussed above. We regard the AllWISE source as a tentative host galaxy identification; this association needs to be confirmed with follow-up work, particularly deep K_ s band imaging.

gmw_00008097.name: J2311 3359
gmw_00008097.overview: J2311 3359 is a radio galaxy [arXiv:2204.08490] characterized by an extremely steep spectrum [arXiv:2204.08490]. The source is unresolved in both ATCA and RACS data, and it is faint at higher frequencies [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: J2311 3359 is described as an extremely steep-spectrum source, faint at higher frequencies, unresolved in ATCA and RACS, but with an ASKAP angular size needing confirmation.

quotes.quote: J2311 3359: This source, not selected from our - criteria (as discussed in Section section: HzRG sample ), has an extremely steep spectrum well described by a single power law with = -1.842 . Therefore, J2311 3359 is faint in our higher-frequency images. The source is unresolved in the ATCA data, but the low S/N does not allow an accurate LAS determination. This source is also unresolved in RACS, but we measured an LAS of 5 6 in an 887.5-MHz ASKAP early science image of the GAMA-23 field see seymour20 . Such an LAS value falls outside of our LAS 5 criterion, but needs to be confirmed with additional radio data.

gmw_00008098.name: J2330 3237
gmw_00008098.overview: J2330 3237 is a radio galaxy interpreted as an asymmetric double-lobed source with unequal lobes [arXiv:2204.08490]. Its western lobe is significantly fainter than the eastern lobe, and there is marginal K_s and H-band identification between these two components [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: The paper interprets J2330 3237 as an asymmetric double with unequal lobes, measured lobe flux densities and spectral indices, possible resolved-out high-frequency flux, and marginal K_s/H-band identification.

quotes.quote: J2330 3237: We interpret this source as an asymmetric double with LAS 4 2, where the components have significantly different flux densities (the western lobe being much fainter). Using the available high-resolution radio data, the flux densities of the brighter eastern lobe are 6.3 0.7 , 3.74 0.27 and 2.11 0.16 mJy at 3, 5.5 and 9 GHz, respectively. Similarly, the values for the significantly fainter component to the west are <0.45 , 0.22 0.06 and <0.078 mJy, respectively (upper limits at the 3 level).

Given that the western lobe is detected at 5.5 GHz only, we verified that this source was not spuriously created as a result of our phase-only self-calibration step. The two point spectral indices are ^ 5500 _ 3000 = -0.86 0.22 and ^ 9000 _ 5500 = -1.16 0.21 for the eastern lobe; similarly these values are ^ 5500 _ 3000 1.2 and ^ 9000 _ 5500 2.1 for the western lobe. The significant spectral steepening of the western lobe at higher frequencies could at least be partly due to flux density possibly being resolved out in the higher-resolution 9-GHz map. Further evidence in favour of a double-lobed morphology is the hint of a K_ s band identification between the two components (brightest pixel value = 3.7 ); there is similar marginal evidence in H band (brightest pixel value = 4.4 ).

gmw_00008099.name: J2340 3230
gmw_00008099.overview: J2340 3230 is a high-redshift radio galaxy candidate [arXiv:2204.08490]. The system features two visible radio sources, with the brighter south-western source assumed to be the primary candidate and a nearby north-eastern source showing tentative evidence of a gigahertz-frequency turnover [arXiv:2204.08490].

sources.summary: The paper assumes J2340 3230 is the brighter south-western HzRG candidate, notes a nearby north-eastern source with tentative GHz turnover, and calls for deeper K_s imaging.

quotes.quote: J2340 3230: Two radio sources are visible, with the north-eastern source having a possible marginal (brightest pixel value = 4.6 ) K_ s band detection in SHARKS. The radio flux densities of this source are 0.75, 0.79 0.11 and 0.58 0.07 mJy at 3, 5.5 and 9 GHz, respectively. Therefore, there is tentative evidence that this source is turning over at GHz frequencies.

For the purpose of analysis in this paper, we have assumed that the HzRG candidate is the south-western source (which is e.g. an order of magnitude brighter at 5.5 GHz) and that the north-eastern source is an unrelated source that is nearby in projection. Alternatively, this could be a lower-redshift source with a one-sided jet (assuming that one of the two sources is the radio core), where the LAS is 6 2. A deeper K_ s band image is needed to identify a possible near-infrared counterpart coincident with the south-western source; there is tentative evidence of a SHARKS K_ s band detection (brightest pixel value = 3.8 ).

gmw_00008100.name: SDSS J144845.91+101010.5
gmw_00008100.overview: SDSS J144845.91+101010.5 is a post-starburst galaxy at a redshift of z=0.646 [arXiv:2208.13917]. It is a nearly-concluded galaxy merger featuring vast molecular tidal tails that contain approximately half of the system's cold gas [arXiv:2208.13917].

sources.summary: The paper reports SDSS J144845.91+101010.5 has vast molecular tidal tails containing about half its cold gas, plausibly quenching star formation after a near-final merger.

quotes.quote: Here we present the discovery of vast molecular tidal gas features reaching out from SDSS J144845.91+101010.5 (SDSS J1448+1010), a z=0.646 post-starburst galaxy from the survey. The cold gas contained in the tidal tails represents about half the cold gas in the entire system, and the nearly-concluded merger and accompanying tidal removal of the star-forming fuel is plausibly the proximate cause of the suppressed star formation in this galaxy.

At the redshift of SDSS J1448+1010, these features are separated from the main galaxy by physical distances on the sky up to 64 kpc. The northern features are mostly blueshifted by 100--150 and the southern feature redshifted by 100--150 . There is no discernible coherent velocity gradient in the gas within the central galaxy. We also explored more aggressive visibility weighting schemes that improve resolution at the expense of sensitivity and found no evidence of ordered motion at 0.8'' resolution. In contrast to the high velocity dispersion observed in the gas located within the central galaxy, _ CO = 290 20 , both extended features are narrow, with dispersion < 50 at all locations. The gas within the central galaxy appears to be genuinely dispersion dominated -- it is implausible that inclination or resolution effects would result in such a high dispersion and lack of velocity gradient for a rotating system. From the /WFC3 and Gemini/FLAMINGOS-2 imaging (Fig. fig:nirobs ), it is clear that the extended CO features are embedded within prominent stellar tidal features. Extended tidal tails such as these are produced in the late stages of galaxy-galaxy mergers with near-equal mass ratio between the merging partners e.g. toomre72,cox08 . The spatial coincidence between the stellar and gas features indicates that the latter are also tidal in origin.

gmw_00008101.name: Object 319
gmw_00008101.overview: Object 319 is a high-redshift galaxy with a photometric redshift of 2.4 to 2.5 [arXiv:0709.2046]. Its spectral energy distribution is best fitted by templates of unreddened elliptical galaxies or maximally old simple stellar populations, although these templates do not explain its detected 24-micron flux [arXiv:0709.2046].

sources.summary: The paper finds Object 319 is a high-redshift I-dropout, best fit by unreddened elliptical or maximally old burst templates, though those SEDs do not explain its 24 micron flux.

quotes.quote: Object 319, one of the two newly identified I drop-outs, has the highest photometric redshift, 2.4--2.5, which is well constrained by the ``curvature'' measured in the IRAC bands due to the stellar peak at 1.6 \ (restframe). Except possibly for 319, none of these objects are detected with MIPS at 24 . Object 319, is best fitted without extinction and with templates of elliptical galaxies or maximally old simple stellar populations (bursts). From SED fitting, this object is thus best characterised as ``elliptical'' in agreement with its empirical classification (Fig. eros1 ). However, these SEDs are not able to explain the 24 \ flux.

gmw_00008102.name: R dropout 1087
gmw_00008102.overview: R dropout 1087 is an infrared galaxy with a spectral energy distribution that declines rapidly between the near-infrared and optical bands [arXiv:0709.2046]. Although a high-redshift Lyman-break fit was initially considered, its photometric redshift is better fit at approximately 0.9 using GRASIL templates [arXiv:0709.2046].

sources.summary: The paper treats R dropout 1087 as having a steep near-IR to optical SED; a formal high-z Lyman-break fit is rejected in favor of a GRASIL fit at about z=0.9.

quotes.quote: The exception is the R dropout 1087, whose SED shows a very rapid and strong decline between the near-IR and optical bands, which is better fit with a Lyman-break than with a Balmer break. Formally its best photometric redshift is thus 7. , but given the brightness of this object at near-IR and Spitzer bands the high- z solution is extremely unlikely. Using the semi-empirical GRASIL templates the best fit is found at 0.9 . This solution also naturally explains the observed 24 \ flux of this object, as shown in Fig. \ fig_eroac114 .

gmw_00008103.overview: The galaxy 632 is the brightest extremely red object in its sample and is not detected at 24 microns [arXiv:0709.2046]. It exhibits a burst-like star formation history with low to zero extinction, resembling an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:0709.2046].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 632 as the brightest ERO, non-detected at 24 microns, with low extinction and burst-like history resembling an elliptical; template fits give redshifts 1.3 or 2.5.

quotes.quote: For 4 objects ( 512, 632, 680, 707) the SED fitting indicates relatively small amounts of extinction ( 0.6). Interestingly these correspond precisely to all the objects which are non-detected at 24 . From their star formation history (all burst-like) and their low (but not zero?) extinction these objects resemble ellipticals, in agreement with their empirical classification. For the brightest of all our EROs, 632, we obtain different solutions with the BC and S04gyr templates respectively, namely 1.3 (2.5), ages of 4.5 (2.6) Gyr, and little or no extinction, 0.6 (0.).

gmw_00008104.name: KURVS-1
gmw_00008104.overview: KURVS-1 is a compact, nearly spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:2305.04382]. It exhibits dispersion-dominated dynamics with high velocity dispersion, a somewhat regular inner velocity field, and large-radius asymmetries that may suggest a secondary component [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-1 is described as compact and nearly spheroidal, with a somewhat regular inner velocity field, large-radius asymmetries suggesting a secondary component, and dispersion-dominated dynamics.

quotes.quote: KURVS-1 or cdfs 24904. This is a compact, nearly spheroidal galaxy, as suggested by the HST high-resolution imaging and measurements of the near-infrared half-light radius. KURVS ultra-deep observations show a somewhat regular velocity field in the inner regions of the galaxy as well as some asymmetries at large radii possibly suggesting the presence of a secondary component. The galaxy shows high velocity dispersion and dispersion-dominated dynamics.

gmw_00008105.name: KURVS-2
gmw_00008105.overview: KURVS-2 is a large, nearly face-on spiral galaxy characterized by a clumpy structure [arXiv:2305.04382]. Although it displays a mostly regular velocity field, it is excluded from the disc sample due to insufficient rotational support [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-2 is a large nearly face-on clumpy spiral with H-flux clumps, a mostly regular but asymmetric KMOS velocity field, uncertain outer perturbations, and insufficient rotational support for the disc sample.

quotes.quote: KURVS-2 or cdfs 26404. The HST imaging indicates that this is a large clumpy spiral galaxy nearly face-on. Clumps are also observed in the H flux map. KMOS observations indicate a regular velocity field with some asymmetries at both edges of the rotation curve. However, the outer regions of the rotation curve are associated with large uncertainties, and it is difficult to establish if these asymmetries are tracing perturbations at large radii. The galaxy is overall regularly rotating, but we exclude it from the ``discs sample'' because of its v_ rot / _0 ratio.

gmw_00008106.name: KURVS-3
gmw_00008106.overview: KURVS-3 is a massive spiral galaxy that features a central bulge visible in Hubble Space Telescope imaging [arXiv:2305.04382]. It exhibits rotationally supported dynamics characterized by a regular velocity field, a centrally peaked velocity dispersion, and a declining rotation curve [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-3 is a massive spiral with a prominent bulge, regular velocity field, centrally peaked dispersion, rotationally supported dynamics, and a significantly declining rotation curve.

quotes.quote: KURVS-3 or cdfs 26954. This is a massive spiral galaxy with a prominent bulge in the HST imaging. The galaxy has a regular velocity field and centrally-peaked velocity dispersion profile, suggesting rotationally-supported dynamics. This is also reflected in the v_ rot / _0 ratio, which is well above unity for this galaxy. The galaxy shows a declining rotation curve at 3 significance.

gmw_00008107.name: KURVS-4
gmw_00008107.overview: KURVS-4 is a spiral galaxy featuring a bright central bulge and a nearly face-on orientation [arXiv:2305.04382]. It exhibits highly perturbed velocity and velocity dispersion maps, a declining rotation curve at large radii, and a possible companion galaxy at a large distance [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-4 is a bright-bulged spiral with a possible companion, highly perturbed velocity and dispersion maps, near face-on geometry, and a significantly declining but uncertain rotation curve.

quotes.quote: KURVS-4 or cdfs 27318. This spiral galaxy shows a bright bulge in the HST imaging. The HST image also shows indication of a possible companion at large radius, beyond the extent of the cut-out shown in Figure figA3:kurvs_dataset4 . The velocity map and velocity dispersion are highly perturbed, and the small velocity gradient indicate that the galaxy is nearly face on. The highly-perturbed nature of this galaxy is also indicated by v_ rot / _0 0.2 . The rotation curve of this galaxy is significantly declining at large radii.

gmw_00008108.name: KURVS-5
gmw_00008108.overview: KURVS-5 is a clumpy galaxy that exhibits a regular but asymmetric velocity field [arXiv:2305.04382]. The galaxy also displays an asymmetric velocity dispersion profile and has a rotation-to-dispersion ratio too low to be classified as a disc [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-5 is described as a clumpy galaxy with regular but asymmetric velocity structure, asymmetric dispersion, and kinematic support too low for the paper's disc sample.

quotes.quote: KURVS-5 or cdfs 28138. This is a clumpy galaxy with a regular but asymmetric velocity field. The galaxy also shows an asymmetric velocity dispersion profile. We exclude it from the ``discs sample'' because of its v_ rot / _0 ratio.

gmw_00008109.name: KURVS-6
gmw_00008109.overview: KURVS-6 is a low-inclination spiral galaxy characterized by a bright central component and spiral arms [arXiv:2305.04382]. Its velocity map shows regular rotation, while its velocity dispersion map is centrally peaked [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-6 is a low-inclination spiral with central structure and arms, regular rotation, centrally peaked dispersion, and edge pixels associated with a broad high-dispersion component.

quotes.quote: KURVS-6 or cdfs 29207. The HST morphology suggests a low-inclination spiral galaxy with a bright central component and spiral arms. The velocity map indicates regular rotation, and the velocity dispersion map is centrally peaked. There are a few ``noisy'' pixels at the edge of both the rotation curve and the velocity dispersion profile. From a visual inspection of the KMOS cube, these pixels seems to be associated with a broad component having high velocity dispersion. Excluding these pixels from the fit to the rotation curve does not change the outer rotation curve shape.

gmw_00008110.name: KURVS-7
gmw_00008110.overview: KURVS-7 is a highly inclined spiral galaxy that exhibits regular rotation [arXiv:2305.04382]. Its dynamics are rotationally supported, featuring a centrally peaked velocity dispersion profile and a somewhat asymmetric rotation curve [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-7 is a highly inclined spiral with regular rotation, a somewhat asymmetric rotation curve, centrally peaked dispersion, and rotationally supported dynamics.

quotes.quote: KURVS-7 or cdfs 29589. This is a highly-inclined spiral galaxy. The velocity field suggests regular rotation, although the rotation curve is somewhat asymmetric. The velocity dispersion profile is centrally peaked. This suggests that the galaxy is rotationally-supported in its dynamics, as also indicated by the v_ rot / _0 ratio.

gmw_00008111.name: KURVS-8
gmw_00008111.overview: KURVS-8 is a small, clumpy galaxy [arXiv:2305.04382]. Observations reveal rotationally-supported dynamics, characterized by a regular velocity field, a centrally-peaked velocity dispersion profile, and a declining outer rotation curve [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-8 is a small clumpy galaxy whose KURVS observations indicate rotational support through regular velocity structure, central dispersion, and a significantly declining outer rotation curve.

quotes.quote: KURVS-8 or cdfs 29831. The HST images indicate a small, clumpy galaxy. KURVS observations and measurements of the v_ rot / _0 ratio indicate rotationally-supported dynamics, as suggested by a regular velocity field and centrally-peaked velocity dispersion profile. The galaxy shows a declining outer rotation curve at 3 significance.

gmw_00008112.name: KURVS-10
gmw_00008112.overview: KURVS-10 is formally classified as a rotationally-supported disc galaxy based on its regularly-rotating velocity field [arXiv:2305.04382]. However, high-resolution imaging and an asymmetric rotation curve suggest that its kinematic signatures actually arise from the orbital motion of a merging pair [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-10 appears formally disc-like in KURVS kinematics, but HST imaging and asymmetric dispersion favor interpreting its signal as a merging pair rather than a rotating disc.

quotes.quote: KURVS-10 or cdfs 30450. This galaxy is formally classified as a rotationally-supported disc as it displays a regularly-rotating velocity field, a continuous velocity gradient along the kinematic major axis, has v_ rot / _ 0 1.5 and the position of the steepest velocity gradient coincides with the peak of the velocity dispersion. However, the HST imaging indicates the presence of two merging companions. This suggests that the kinematics signatures observed in KURVS data are associated with the orbital motion of a merging pair, rather than with a rotating disc. This is expected, given that that there is a high probability of misclassifying merging pairs as discs in seeing-limited observations Simons19, Rizzo22 . The highly asymmetric rotation curve as well as the high velocity dispersion measured in the inner regions of the velocity dispersion map support the merging pair interpretation.

gmw_00008113.name: KURVS-12
gmw_00008113.overview: KURVS-12 is a red, compact galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus, as indicated by its infrared colors and X-ray counterpart [arXiv:2305.04382]. The galaxy exhibits highly asymmetric kinematics and perturbed rotation, with its outer regions associated with a broad component [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-12 has infrared and X-ray AGN indicators, red compact HST morphology, highly asymmetric kinematics, a broad outer component, and uncertain but significantly declining outer rotation.

quotes.quote: Of the 22 sources in the KURVS-CDFS sample, we identify two galaxies (KURVS-11 and KURVS-12) whose infrared colours indicate the presence of an AGN. KURVS-12 also has a X-ray counterpart and an intrinsic 0.5 -- 7.0 kev luminosity of L_ X-ray, int = 5 10^ 42 \ erg \ s ^ 1 in the Luo17 catalogue, which also supports the presence of an AGN.

KURVS-12 or cdfs 30561. This is a red, compact galaxy in the HST images. The rotation curve and velocity dispersion profile are highly asymmetric. A visual inspection of the KMOS cube suggests that the outer regions of the kinematic maps are associated with a broad component. Excluding these pixels from the fit to the rotation curve does not change the outer rotation curve shape. The galaxy is not included in the ``discs sample'' because of its clearly perturbed kinematics, which also implies a v_ rot / _0 ratio below our ``discs sample'' cut.

gmw_00008114.name: KURVS-13
gmw_00008114.overview: KURVS-13 is a clumpy spiral galaxy that is classified as a rotating disc [arXiv:2305.04382]. It displays a regular velocity field with a somewhat asymmetric rotation curve and a velocity dispersion profile that peaks at its center [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-13 is a clumpy spiral with regular but somewhat asymmetric rotation, central dispersion, a flat outer dispersion profile within uncertainties, and inclusion in the disc sample.

quotes.quote: KURVS-13 or cdfs 30732. This galaxy is a clumpy spiral according to the HST images. The source displays a regular velocity field and somewhat asymmetric rotation curve. The velocity dispersion profile is peaked at the centre, and noisy and irregular towards the outer regions. However, the outer velocity dispersion profile is consistent with being flat within the uncertainties. The velocity and velocity dispersion maps suggest a rotating disc, and the galaxy is included in the ``discs sample'' given its v_ rot / _0 ratio.

gmw_00008115.name: KURVS-14
gmw_00008115.overview: KURVS-14 is a clumpy galaxy characterized by a regular velocity field and rotation curve [arXiv:2305.04382]. However, it exhibits an asymmetric velocity dispersion profile and a high velocity dispersion, which excludes it from the disc sample [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-14 is a clumpy galaxy with a regular velocity field and rotation curve, but asymmetric high velocity dispersion keeps it out of the disc sample.

quotes.quote: KURVS-14 or cdfs 30865. This is a clumpy galaxy with a regular velocity field and rotation curve. The galaxy also shows an asymmetric velocity dispersion profile, and a high velocity dispersion. As a result, the galaxy is not included in the ``discs sample''.

gmw_00008116.name: KURVS-15
gmw_00008116.overview: KURVS-15 is a clumpy galaxy characterized by a regular velocity field and a symmetric rotation curve [arXiv:2305.04382]. Its kinematic maps suggest a rotation-dominated disc, featuring a velocity dispersion profile that peaks at the center with some outer asymmetries [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-15 is a clumpy galaxy with regular velocity and symmetric rotation curves, central dispersion with outer asymmetries, and kinematic maps consistent with a rotation-dominated disc.

quotes.quote: KURVS-15 or cdfs 31127. A clumpy galaxy with a regular velocity field and symmetric rotation curve. The velocity dispersion profile is peaked at the centre, although it shows some asymmetries in the outer regions. The kinematic maps suggest a rotation-dominated disc, consistent with the v_ rot / _0 ratio.

gmw_00008117.name: KURVS-16
gmw_00008117.overview: KURVS-16 is a clumpy, edge-on disk galaxy [arXiv:2305.04382]. It exhibits a regular velocity field and a centrally peaked velocity dispersion profile, indicating that its dynamics are rotationally supported [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-16 is a clumpy edge-on disc with regular velocity and rotation structure, centrally peaked dispersion, high rotational support, and edge pixels that do not affect the outer curve.

quotes.quote: KURVS-16 or cdfs 31671. The HST imaging indicates a clumpy, edge-on disc. The galaxy shows a regular velocity field and rotation curve, and a centrally peaked velocity dispersion profile. This suggests that the galaxy is rotationally-supported in its dynamics, as also indicated by the high v_ rot / _0 ratio. From a visual inspection of the KMOS cube, there are a few ``noisy'' pixels at the edge of the rotation curve and the velocity dispersion profile. Excluding these pixels from the fit to the rotation curve does not change the outer rotation curve shape.

gmw_00008118.name: KURVS-17
gmw_00008118.overview: KURVS-17 is a clumpy galaxy imaged by the Hubble Space Telescope [arXiv:2305.04382]. It exhibits a regular velocity field and rotation curve, alongside a velocity dispersion profile characterized by a central peak and asymmetric outer regions [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-17 is clumpy, with regular velocity and rotation curves, clipped noisy edge pixels, a central dispersion peak, and uncertain asymmetric outer dispersion.

quotes.quote: KURVS-17 or cdfs 31721. The HST imaging indicates a clumpy galaxy. The object shows a regular velocity field and rotation curve, with a few ``noisy'' pixels at the edge. Clipping these pixels does not significantly affect measurements of the the outer rotation curve shape. The velocity dispersion profile shows a central peak and some asymmetries towards the outer regions. However, the outer pixels have large uncertainties.

gmw_00008119.name: KURVS-18
gmw_00008119.overview: KURVS-18 is an irregular galaxy that exhibits overall rotation [arXiv:2305.04382]. However, its outer kinematics display symmetric disturbances and a perturbed velocity dispersion map, which may indicate ongoing interactions with a companion galaxy [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-18 is an irregular galaxy with overall rotation, symmetric outer disturbances, perturbed dispersion structure, possible companion interaction, and too little rotational support for the disc sample.

quotes.quote: KURVS-18 or cdfs 33014. The HST morphology suggests an irregular galaxy. The velocity map indicates overall rotation. However, the outer rotation curve displays symmetric disturbances. The object presents a perturbed velocity dispersion map and an asymmetric velocity dispersion profile. The perturbations in the outer kinematics might indicate interactions with a companion.

gmw_00008120.name: KURVS-19
gmw_00008120.overview: KURVS-19 is a small, clumpy galaxy that exhibits a high velocity dispersion [arXiv:2305.04382]. It features an asymmetric velocity field and has low rotational support, which excludes it from disc-like classifications [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-19 is a small clumpy galaxy with asymmetric velocity and dispersion maps, high velocity dispersion, stable outer-curve shape after clipping noisy pixels, and low rotational support.

quotes.quote: KURVS-19 or cdfs 33246. The HST image shows a small, clumpy galaxy. KMOS observations indicate an asymmetric velocity field and velocity dispersion profile. The galaxy has a high velocity dispersion. Excluding ``noisy'' pixels at the edge of the rotation curve and the velocity dispersion profile does not significantly change the outer rotation curve shape. The galaxy is excluded from the ``disc sample'' because of its low v_ rot / _0 ratio.

gmw_00008121.name: KURVS-20
gmw_00008121.overview: KURVS-20 is a compact galaxy characterized by a small half-light radius [arXiv:2305.04382]. It features two round-shaped, low-inclination components that may represent interacting structures or clumpy emission, alongside an asymmetric rotation curve and low rotational support that suggest possible merging activity [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-20 is compact, with two low-inclination components, asymmetric rotation, irregular flat dispersion, possible merging activity, and low rotational support.

quotes.quote: KURVS-20 or cdfs 35213. This is a compact galaxy, as suggested by the small half-light radius. The HST image indicates two round-shaped components with low inclinations, it is unclear if these are interacting or are tracing clumpy emission. The object shows a highly asymmetric rotation curve and an irregular velocity dispersion profile, nearly flat. The kinematic properties of the source might also indicate merging activity. The irregular nature of this galaxy is also reflected in the low v_ rot / _0 ratio.

gmw_00008122.name: KURVS-21
gmw_00008122.overview: KURVS-21 is a compact, massive galaxy characterized by a central bulge and blue emission at its outer edges [arXiv:2305.04382]. Although classified as a rotation-dominated disk galaxy, it exhibits asymmetric kinematics and a perturbed rotation curve [arXiv:2305.04382].

sources.summary: KURVS-21 is compact and bulge-dominated with asymmetric kinematics; its effective-radius dark matter fraction is flagged as uncertain, while notes classify it as rotation-dominated but perturbed.

quotes.quote: Finally, one galaxy, KURVS-21, formally has zero dark matter fraction at the effective radius. This object is a compact and massive galaxy with a prominent bulge and an asymmetric rotation curve which is difficult to reproduce accurately with galpak ^ 3D . As a result, the dark matter fraction for this object is uncertain and we flag the measurement in the plot.

KURVS-21 or GS4 10784. The HST image indicates a bulgy galaxy with blue emission at the edges. It is unclear if this emission traces spiral arms or disturbances. The rotation curve and velocity dispersion profile are asymmetric. There are issues with measurements of the radial and velocity offset obtained from fitting the 1D rotation curve. The object is formally rotation-dominated given the high v_ rot / _0 ratio, and we include it in our `discs sample'.

gmw_00008123.name: 3C 336
gmw_00008123.overview: The object 3C 336 is a lobe-dominated, radio-loud quasar with a steep spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0603591]. It features a radio jet extending to the southeast and is located in a region with several associated cluster galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0603591].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 336 as a lobe-dominated radio-loud quasar with extended [O II] emission partly aligned with its radio jet and possible nearby cluster galaxies.

quotes.quote: 3C 336 3C 336 is a steep-spectrum lobe-dominated RLQ with radio lobes separated by 28 \ at PA 30 ^ and a jet extending to the south-east of the QSO pooley74,bridle94 . Both bremer92b and steidel97 reported extended [ O ii ] emission at z=0.927 to the north and south of the QSO. This emission, detected out to a distance of 5 \ from the core, was previously interpreted as due to a cooling flow bremer92b , while steidel97 found that it belonged to a blob without a continuum counterpart. The quasar is located in a region where many galaxies are present close to the sight-line. Most of the galaxies are intervening, but seven galaxies have redshifts consistent with being associated with a cluster at z=0.923 , i.e. at the quasar redshift steidel97 .

gmw_00008124.name: Maffei 2
gmw_00008124.overview: Maffei 2 is a nearby spiral galaxy that hosts a central bar with a two- to four-component structure [arXiv:1302.3526]. This central region contains dense gas structures, including a high-temperature component and two distinct velocity components detected in formaldehyde absorption [arXiv:1302.3526].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Maffei 2 central-bar molecular structures, reports first H2CO measurements in the galaxy, and resolves the K-doublet spectra into two velocity components.

quotes.quote: Maffei 2: Images of the CO J= 1-0 , 2-1 , and CS J= 2-1 emission Kuno2008 , ^ 13 CO and C ^ 18 O J= 1-0 and 2-1 and HCN J= 1-0 emission Meier2008 , and NH _3 (1,1) and (2,2) emission Lebron2011 all point to a 5^ 30^ two- to four-component structure of the central bar in this nearby spiral galaxy. These studies indicate dense gas structures with spatial densities and kinetic temperatures 10^4 cm ^ 3 and T _K 30-40 K, respectively. High-excitation NH _3 (6,6) measurements Mauersberger2003 have uncovered a high-temperature (T _K 130 K) component in this galaxy. Our detections of H _2 CO 1_ 10 1_ 11 and 2_ 11 2_ 12 absorption (Figure fig:NGC253NGC660Maffei2IC342FormSpec ) are the first reported measurements of H _2 CO in this galaxy. The K-doublet H _2 CO spectra are clearly composed of two velocity components at 98 (component ``C1'') and +25 km s ^ 1 (component ``C2''), consistent with previous studies Mauersberger2003 .

gmw_00008125.name: IR 15107+0724
gmw_00008125.overview: IR 15107+0724 is a galaxy that exhibits compact nuclear carbon monoxide emission [arXiv:1302.3526]. Observations of the galaxy have also detected formaldehyde in absorption [arXiv:1302.3526].

sources.summary: The paper says IR 15107+0724 has a 1_10-1_11 measurement consistent with previous work, a 2_11-2_12 absorption detection, and compact nuclear CO emission.

quotes.quote: IR 15107+0724: Our 1_ 10 1_ 11 measurement is consistent with that reported by Baan1993 and Araya2004 . We also detect the 2_ 11 2_ 12 line in absorption (Figure fig:NGC3628IC860M83IR15107FormSpec ). Planesas1991 imaged the CO J= 1-0 emission from this galaxy, deriving a compact nuclear source with size 3^ .

gmw_00008126.name: HERS1
gmw_00008126.overview: HERS1 is a gravitationally lensed galaxy that was first identified by the SpaceWarps citizen science project [arXiv:1601.03401]. It is the brightest source in its catalog at 500 microns and is also detected in the Planck all-sky point source catalog [arXiv:1601.03401].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HERS1 as a lensed galaxy, the brightest 500 micron source in the catalog, with visible K-band lensing and Planck-supported far-infrared SED measurements.

quotes.quote: HERS1, shown in Figure 2, had been already identified as a lensed galaxy by a citizen science group SpaceWarps making use of VISTA-CFHT optical imaging data in SDSS Stripe 82 Geach2015 . It is the brightest target in our list of 77 with a 500 m flux density of 717 mJy. It is a unique source in that it stands out at the bright-end separately on its own while the next set of lensed candidate sources have flux densities well below 300 mJy. The Keck/NIRC2 LGS-AO HELMS Deep K _s band we present in Figure 2, obtained prior to us knowing about the SpaceWarps project and the Geach2015 publication, has substantially better resolution than VISTA-CFHT K_s band image in Geach2015 . Unlike our previous lens follow-up programs in the K band that resulted in the need for deep integrations due to faintness of the lensed source, HERS1 is bright enough in the K band that the lensing galaxy is easily visible in a single NIRC2 frame of 60 seconds.

Additional multi-wavelength observations of this target, including CO spectroscopy, is reported in Geach2015 . HERS1 is also detected in Planck all-sky point source catalog and is identified as PCCS2 857 G160.58-56.76 in the Planck 857 GHz point source catalog with a separation of less than 1 arcminute from the Herschel /SPIRE 250 m position, which is smaller than full-width-half-maximum (FWHM) resolution of the Planck ( 4^ ) at 857 GHz. The Planck has a 857 GHz flux of 1118 408 mJy and a 545 GHz flux of 676 197 mJy.

gmw_00008127.name: HELMS6
gmw_00008127.overview: HELMS6 is a high-redshift galaxy that is strongly gravitationally lensed [arXiv:1601.03401]. The system features giant arcs and a main lensing galaxy that belongs to a cluster of galaxies at a redshift of approximately 0.396 [arXiv:1601.03401].

sources.summary: The paper shows HELMS6 K-band imaging with a cluster-member lensing galaxy, giant arcs, and GTC spectroscopy measuring three cluster-galaxy redshifts near z=0.396.

quotes.quote: Figure 2 also shows the WHT/LIRIS K_s imaging of HELMS6 observed on 26 October 2015, with seeing of 0.8 '' . In this case, the main lensing galaxy is a member of a cluster of galaxies, with SDSS photometric redshift 0.4 . Giant arcs are also present in this system.
From these observations we could measure the spectroscopic redshifts of three galaxies in the cluster, of 0.3950, 0.3956, and 0.3968, close to the SDSS photometric redshift.

gmw_00008128.name: HELMS29
gmw_00008128.overview: HELMS29 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=5.162 [arXiv:1601.03401]. It is a strongly lensed star-forming galaxy that was detected by the Atacama Cosmology Telescope [arXiv:1601.03401].

sources.summary: The paper reports HELMS29 as an ACT-detected lensed galaxy with CO redshift z=5.162, the highest-redshift lensing galaxy in the sample, and fits its SED with ACT data.

quotes.quote: Two of the ACT detected lensed galaxies in our catalog (HERS1 and HELMS29) have spectroscopic redshift from CO observations (Harris et al in prep, Riechers et al in prep). HELMS29 at z=5.162 (see below) is the highest redshift lensing galaxy in our sample. We use this redshift along with the Herschel far-infrared and ACT mm data to construct the SED (Figure 6). Similar to HERS1, discussed above, we treated as a free parameter in the SED fit of HELMS29. The addition of ACT data provided a better fit to the flux densities at longer wavelengths with a =2.6 (for more details on the fitting procedure see: Casey2012a ).

gmw_00008129.name: ISO A2218 02
gmw_00008129.overview: ISO A2218 02 is an infrared galaxy detected in both the LW2 and LW3 bands [arXiv:astro-ph/0406391]. It is identified with the optical counterpart L664 at a redshift of z=0.53, though the nearby galaxy L663 may also contribute to its mid-infrared flux [arXiv:astro-ph/0406391].

sources.summary: ISO A2218 67 is a two-band MIR source identified with L75/S4004, with possible neighboring contributions; a multi-counterpart fit still underestimates LW3 and its X-ray detection may indicate dusty-torus AGN emission.

quotes.quote: ISO A2218 67. This ISOCAM source is detected in both the LW2 and LW3 bands. It is identified with L75 (also S4004), a faint object at unknown z . Other galaxies could contribute to the MIR emission, in particular L71 (also S4005), an Scm, but possibly also L67.

The formal best-fit is obtained for a combination of a z=1.1 El5 model, and a z=2.8 Es2 model. Although the fit now reproduces the LW2 flux, it still underestimates the LW3 flux. The role of another possible counterpart, L67, cannot be evaluated for lack of photometric data. Notably, this source has also been detected in the X-ray by Chandra (Sanchez, private comm.). Hence, part of the MIR emission could arise from an AGN component embedded in a dusty torus, something that GRASIL models do not currently account for.

sources.summary: The paper searches for CO in the lensed Lyman-break galaxy A2218 384, reports no detection, and sets upper limits on CO luminosity and gas mass.

quotes.quote: We set a gas mass limit of 3 <6 10^8 M _ for the Lyman-break galaxy, A2218 384, located in the same field as SMM J16359 at z=2.515 .
We also search for \ \ emission in (iv) the Lyman-break galaxy (LBG) A 2218 284, located 22.1 arcsec from the centre of the lensing cluster in the same field as SMM J16359 ebbels96 .

The Lyman-break galaxy, Abell 2218 384 Returning to the observations for SMM J16359, we searched for \ \ in the LBG at R.A. \ 16:35:49.4, Dec. \ 66:13:07 J2000; ebbels96 located behind the same cluster. We do not detect this source, but set an upper limit using S 3 _ n v w seaquist95 , where v is the velocity resolution of the spectrum, w 250 \ is a typical LBG line width greve08 and the noise in the primary beam-corrected VLA spectrum is _ n . We de-boost this by 16 to account for gravitational lensing by the cluster ebbels96 and thus derive upper limits on the \ \ luminosity of < 0.8 10^ 9 Jy km s ^ 1 pc ^ 2 and on the gas mass of <6 10^ 8 , respectively, where we have assumed X_ CO = 0.8 , supposedly appropriate for star-forming galaxies.

sources.summary: ISO A2218 02 is a two-band ISOCAM source associated with L664 at z=0.53; its optical-to-MIR SED is poorly fit, possibly because nearby L663 contributes MIR flux.

quotes.quote: ISO A2218 02. This ISOCAM source is detected in both the LW2 and LW3 bands. We identify this ISOCAM source with the optical counterpart L664, at a redshift z=0.53 , but we note that the nearby galaxy L663, at unknown redshift, could contribute substantially to the MIR flux, since its band flux density is 30 of the flux density of L664. Using L664 as the only optical counterpart, we do not find acceptable fits to the source optical-to-MIR SED. Formally, the best-fit is obtained for a Sa5 model, but the fit is poor mostly because the model underestimates the B band flux. It is possible that the MIR flux is provided by both L664 and L663.

sources.summary: ISO A2218 28 is associated with the cluster cD L391 at z=0.1720; its extended MIR emission and crowded surroundings make its SED difficult to fit with L391 alone.

quotes.quote: ISO A2218 28. This ISOCAM source is detected in the LW2 band only, and its optical counterpart is L391, the cluster cD, at z=0.1720 . Its spectral type is that of an early-type galaxy, according to LPS. The MIR emission of this ISOCAM source is very extended, and probably it is not contributed solely by the cD (see Fig. f-imag1 ). Using L391 as the only optical counterpart, this source SED is best fit by an Em10 model, but the fit is poor, mainly because it underestimates the K_s band flux. Given the very extended emission from this galaxy, and the considerable crowding of galaxies in its vicinity, it is perhaps not surprising that it is difficult to fit its SED.

sources.summary: ISO A2218 53 is an LW3-only source likely associated with L205/S368, an Sc galaxy at z=0.693, but possible L212 contamination leaves its two-galaxy SED fit unconstrained.

quotes.quote: ISO A2218 53. This ISOCAM source is detected in the LW3 band only. Its most likely optical counterpart is L205 (also S368), but L212 might contribute significantly to the MIR flux, since the band flux ratio of the two galaxies is 0.4. L205 is an Sc at z=0.693 , L212 is an unknown- z spiral. Using L205 as the only optical counterpart, the best-fit is obtained for a Sc5 model, but the fit is poor, mainly because it underestimates the K_s band flux. Unfortunately, we do not have enough data to constrain a fit with two independent models for the two galaxies, and a free redshift for L212.

sources.summary: The paper finds Abell 2218 KESR consistent with simulated high-redshift galaxies, deriving an old stellar population, pronounced Balmer break, and physical parameters that do not challenge current formation models.

quotes.quote: Assuming that Abell 2218 KESR is located at z=6.75 , the SFHs in Figure fig:sfh suggest that it may have formed its first stars before z=15 , with (10 , 50 ) of its stars in place by z= (11,8); in other words, roughly half of the best-fitting models' stars at the epoch of observation are over 150 Myr old. This relatively old population readily accounts for the pronounced Balmer break that is visible in Figure fig:spectra , and is typical in our simulations.

The physical parameters derived for Abell 2218 KESR using our library of simulated galaxies are in line with previous determinations, and those employing various one-parameter SFHs. The formal uncertainties are smaller when using simulated galaxies, because the simulations intrinsically produce a relatively narrow range of SFHs for galaxies at these epochs. The existence of an object with the properties of Abell 2218 KESR at these epochs does not pose a significant challenge to current models of galaxy formation.

sources.summary: The paper identifies a strongly magnified, multiply imaged Lyman-emitting galaxy behind Abell 2218, confirmed spectroscopically and by lens modeling with a low unlensed line luminosity.

quotes.quote: We discovered a source toward Abell 2218 at z=5.6 that we discussed in detail in ell01 . That source is strongly magnified (a factor of 33) and multiply imaged: we used the HST data to locate a second image outside of our survey region. The redshift identification was confirmed by an intermediate-resolution spectrum of both images, showing them to be identical, with P-Cygni line profiles characteristic of Lyman , and certainly not the [ O 2 ] 3727 \ doublet. Our lensing model additionally constrained the redshift of the source to be consistent only with the identification of the line as Lyman . The unlensed luminosity in the \ line is (7.8 0.8) 10^ 41 erg s^ 1 .

sources.summary: ISO A2218 35 is a two-band ISOCAM source identified with the Scd galaxy L317/S420 at z=0.474, whose SED is only marginally fit by the Mps5 model.

quotes.quote: ISO A2218 35. This is an ISOCAM source detected in both the LW2 and LW3 bands. It is identified with L317 (also S420), an Scd at z=0.474 . The best-fit to the source SED is given by a Mps5 model, but the fit is only marginally acceptable (at the 1.6 .l.).

gmw_00008130.name: borg 2134-0708 774
gmw_00008130.overview: borg 2134-0708 774 is a resolved, very red high-redshift dropout candidate with a photometric redshift peaking at z=10.0 [arXiv:1512.05363]. The galaxy exhibits an extended structure [arXiv:1512.05363] and lies in close proximity to a foreground galaxy that may provide modest gravitational lensing [arXiv:1512.05363].

sources.summary: borg 2134-0708 774 is a resolved, very red z~10 dropout candidate with M_AB about 22.2, extended structure, and possible modest lensing from a nearby foreground galaxy.

quotes.quote: borg 2134-0708 774 is a galaxy with magnitude =25.35 and a very red color ( =1.74 ). The photometric redshift probability distribution peaks at z=10.0 , implying M_ AB 22.2 , albeit there is a broad wing of lower z solutions. The source is clearly resolved and shows extended structure in both the and band images. Its intrinsic half-light radius is r_e=0 23 , after correction for the broadening introduced by the Point Spread Function (PSF). Interestingly, the dropout is in close proximity ( 1 46 center to center) to a foreground galaxy of magnitude =23.22.

gmw_00008131.name: borg 2140+0241 37
gmw_00008131.overview: The high-redshift galaxy candidate borg 2140+0241 37 has a photometric redshift estimated at approximately 10.36 [arXiv:2002.06209] and is detected only in the reddest bands of its survey [arXiv:1512.05363]. It exhibits an extended structure [arXiv:1512.05363] and is subject to modest gravitational magnification from a nearby foreground galaxy [arXiv:1512.05363].

sources.summary: The paper identifies par2139+0241 1709 as its highest-redshift candidate, with IRAC deblending limits both favoring z about 10.3 and a final z_best of 10.36.

quotes.quote: Candidate par2139+0241 1709 (left-most column in Figure iracfig ) has a very close neighbor in the F160W image and therefore merits further discussion. The candidate and its neighbor are too close together to successfully deblend their IRAC fluxes. We therefore consider two limiting cases: (1) all IRAC flux belongs to the candidate (middle stamp, indicated by a purple star), and (2) all IRAC flux belongs to the neighbor (right stamp). In both cases, the best-fit EAZY templates prefer high-redshift solutions with z_ best = 10.33 and 10.28 for cases (1) and (2), respectively.

Galaxy candidate par2139+0241 1709 has the highest photometric redshift solution. We obtain two limiting photometric redshifts with the analysis of IRAC data as mentioned in Section irac . We take the average of the two photometric redshift solutions and calculate a final value of z_ best = 10.36, and mag _ H_ 160 = 25.3.

sources.summary: borg 2140+0241 37 is a bright z>10 dropout candidate seen only in the two reddest filters, with extended structure and predicted modest magnification from a nearby foreground lens.

quotes.quote: borg 2140+0241 37 is comparably bright ( =24.94) to borg 2134-0708 774, and it is only detected in the two reddest bands of the survey. Therefore, the photometric redshift has a strong preference for z>10 solutions. Like borg 2134-0708 774, this object is also close in projection ( 1 02 center to center) to a foreground brighter galaxy ( =24.05), also expected to be at z 0.5 because of the compact size, and therefore a potential lens. The lensing magnification predicted by our modeling is =1.2 0.1 , essentially identical to that for borg 2134-0708 774 (see above), because the smaller angular separation compensates for the lower luminosity of the lensing galaxy. The dropout galaxy has an extended structure, especially in the band image ( r_e=0 37 ).

gmw_00008132.name: borg 0956+2848 85
gmw_00008132.overview: The galaxy borg 0956+2848 85 is a faint but confidently detected high-redshift candidate [arXiv:1512.05363]. It has a photometric redshift solution of z=8.7 and exhibits a compact size that disfavors lower-redshift alternatives [arXiv:1512.05363].

sources.summary: borg 0956+2848 85 is the highest-redshift candidate in the sample, a faint but confidently detected z=8.7 dropout whose strong break and compact size disfavor a lower-redshift solution.

quotes.quote: borg 0956+2848 85 is the candidate with the highest photometric redshift solution ( z=8.7 ). Despite being relatively faint ( =26.41) it is confidently detected because of the long exposure times (e.g., 4400s in ). The drop is also the most prominent in the sample ( =2.1 ). Finally, its compact size ( r_e=0 08 after accounting for the PSF) strengthens the rejection of the alternative (already disfavored) photometric redshift solution at z 1.8 .

gmw_00008133.name: borg 2229-0945 548
gmw_00008133.overview: The high-redshift galaxy candidate borg 2229-0945 548 is a dropout galaxy with a photometric redshift peaked at z=8.4 [arXiv:1512.05363]. It lies in close proximity to a brighter, passive foreground galaxy that gravitationally lenses and magnifies its flux [arXiv:1512.05363].

sources.summary: borg 2229-0945 548 is a z=8.4 dropout candidate with a strong F105W break, lying close to a brighter passive galaxy whose foreground deflection may magnify its flux.

quotes.quote: borg 2229-0945 548 has a very significant drop in F105W ( =2.04), which leads to a photo- z distribution sharply peaked at z=8.4 . The dropout galaxy, which has = 25.12, is in very close proximity to a brighter z 2.1 passive galaxy ( =22.58; center to center distance equal to 1 48, see bottom panel in Figure fig:z9 ). Based on modeling of the foreground deflector following baronenugent2015 and , we estimate a gravitational lensing magnification of the dropout flux 1.3 0.3 or 1.9 0.7 , respectively.

gmw_00008134.name: borg 1209+4543 1696
gmw_00008134.overview: The source borg 1209+4543 1696 is a high-redshift galaxy candidate identified as a dropout [arXiv:1512.05363]. It has a photometric redshift distribution that peaks strongly at z=8.56, although its relatively large size suggests a low-redshift solution could be preferred [arXiv:1512.05363].

sources.summary: borg 1209+4543 1696 is an additional z=8.56 dropout candidate omitted from the main catalog because a hot pixel affected segmentation, though the authors still regard it as credible.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, one of these new candidates, borg 1209+4543 1696, is identified as a dropout, with a photometric redshift distribution strongly peaked at z=8.56 and 25.0 . The reason why the source is not in the main catalog is because of the presence of an hot pixel, correctly identified by the reduction pipeline, in the outskirts of the dropout image. The pixel is included in the segmentation map of the F160W+F140W detection image, thereby excluding the galaxy from further analysis owing to our choice to discard any source with one or more pixels having zero weight (infinite rms). With the selection in F160W only, the galaxy segmentation map avoids inclusion of the hot pixel, and SExtractor photometry is performed without incurring in unbound errors. Since the defect is located in the outer parts of the image of the galaxy, we consider this source a credible high- z candidate, albeit its relatively large size would indicate the possibility that the low- z solution is preferred.

gmw_00008135.name: borg 0119-3411 22
gmw_00008135.overview: The source borg 0119-3411 22 is a bright dropout galaxy located near the color-selection boundary for high-redshift objects [arXiv:1512.05363]. Its photometric redshift distribution displays both low-redshift and high-redshift peaks, with the low-redshift solution being marginally preferred [arXiv:1512.05363].

sources.summary: borg 0119-3411 22 is a bright additional dropout near the color-selection boundary, with ambiguous photometric redshift peaks and a marginally preferred low-redshift solution.

quotes.quote: With this selection, we identified one additional dropout borg 0119-3411 22, which is quite bright ( =25.06). In the near-IR color-color space, the source lies close to the boundaries of the selection box for high- z objects. Therefore, it is not surprising that the photometric redshift distribution shows both a low and an high redshift peak (Figure fig:additional_candidates ), with the low- z solution marginally preferred. The uncertainty in the nature of this source is compounded by the relatively short exposure time in F350LP which was constrained by readout conflicts with the primary observations associated to this opportunity.

gmw_00008136.name: northern companion
aliases_external: northern CO-emitting companion of SpARCS1049+56 BCG
gmw_00008136.overview: The northern companion is a CO-emitting galaxy located northeast of a brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:2001.05776]. It is coincident with a merging pair of cluster members and contains a non-negligible amount of molecular gas and stellar mass [arXiv:2001.05776].

sources.summary: The northern companion is a CO-emitting source northeast of the BCG, coincident with a merging pair, offset by +130 km/s, with substantial molecular gas and stellar mass relative to the BCG.

quotes.quote: The intensity map shows the two detected sources, which are about 2 arcsec to the northeast and southeast from the BCG. They also show a clear separation in velocity, being located at +130 km s ^ 1 and 60 km s ^ 1 with respect to the BCG, respectively. The moment 2 map shows similar extensions of 150-250 km s ^ 1 in CO(4 3) for the two sources along the spectral axis.
Interestingly, the northern CO emitting source is coincident with a pair of merging cluster members.

We used the H _2 masses of the northern and southern BCG companions, reported in Sect. sec:result_MH2 , to estimate their stellar mass. Under the assumption that the H _2 to-stellar mass ratio of the two sources reflects that of field galaxies, we can express the ratio as M_ H _2 /M_ 0.1 (1+z)^2 Carilli_Walter2013 . This yields M_ 9.5 10^ 10 M_ and M_ 1.4 10^ 11 M_ for the northern and southern BCG satellites, respectively, that we detected separately in CO. Both molecular gas and stellar components of these BCG companions are, therefore, non-negligible and 0.2 and 0.3 of the total M_ =4.5 10^ 11 M_ of the BCG Webb2015a , respectively.

gmw_00008137.name: southern companion
aliases_external: southern CO-emitting companion of SpARCS1049+56 BCG
gmw_00008137.overview: The southern companion is a satellite galaxy located southeast of the brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:2001.05776]. It features a bent tail of molecular gas that suggests it may be a high-redshift analog of a jellyfish galaxy undergoing ram-pressure stripping [arXiv:2001.05776].

sources.summary: The southern companion is a CO source southeast of the BCG with a molecular-gas tail; the authors favor ram-pressure stripping over tidal interactions and identify it as a high-redshift jellyfish analog.

quotes.quote: The intensity map shows the two detected sources, which are about 2 arcsec to the northeast and southeast from the BCG. They also show a clear separation in velocity, being located at +130 km s ^ 1 and 60 km s ^ 1 with respect to the BCG, respectively.

However, the southern source shows evidence for a southern extension, which is visible from the intensity map. The velocity map also indicates that this southern tail may be receding with a velocity 100 km s ^ 1 with respect to the main source, while the recession seems to disappear at the very end of the tail. The velocity gradient suggests that the southern extension is associated with a bent tail of molecular gas.

It is worth mentioning that we cannot firmly exclude the possibility that the tail is instead due to tidal interactions. However, since we only detected one extended tail, we tend to favor the ram-pressure scenario. Therefore, the southern BCG satellite is possibly the first high- z analog of local jellyfish galaxies e.g., Jachym2014,Jachym2017,Jachym2019,George2018,Vulcani2018,Poggianti2019 as well as of intermediate- z ones, such as those recently discovered in the clusters Abell 1758 z=0.27 , Kalita_Ebeling2019 and CGr32 z=0.73 , Boselli2019 .

gmw_00008138.name: object F
gmw_00008138.overview: Object F is a galaxy that appears physically connected to the filament T2 [arXiv:1206.2680]. It may be a halo member, a remnant of an interacting system, a dwarf galaxy formed at the tip of a tidal tail, or a satellite stripped of gas by ram-pressure [arXiv:1206.2680].

sources.summary: The paper describes object F as a possible halo member at the end of filament T2, perhaps a remnant, a dwarf formed in a tidal tail, or gas-stripped satellite.

quotes.quote: Object F could be the remnant of one of the interacting objects, or a dwarf galaxy that has formed at the tip of tidal tail, as has been proposed for low redshift interacting galaxies (e.g., Schweizer 1978, Mirabel, Dottori Lutz 1992). Young stellar populations in star forming clumps along low z tidal tails have also been observed and studied in considerable detail (e.g., Whitmore Schweizer 1995; Smith et al 2008; Karl et al 2010). In an intriguing alternative scenario, the luminous tail may be gas stripped from object F by ram-pressure when passing through the intergalactic medium, and the increased turbulence may have led to in-situ star formation.

Object F looks like a halo member because it appears physically connected to the filament T2, the emission of which, we have argued, consists mainly of Ly radiation at the same redshift as the main Ly line.

gmw_00008139.name: J1429 + 3356
gmw_00008139.overview: J1429 + 3356 is a giant radio quasar located at a redshift of z=1.12 [arXiv:2109.07825]. The galaxy features a steep optical continuum and a small inclination angle, which results in very little dust contamination of its central active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2109.07825].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1429 + 3356 as a giant radio quasar with multiwavelength data; X-CIGALE SED fitting yields a steep optical continuum and small inclination angle with little dust contamination.

quotes.quote: In our sample we identified two GRQs, J1429 + 3356 ( =14 ^ h 29 ^ m 42.65 ^ s = + 33 ^ 56 ^ 54.8 ^ , z=1.12) and J1609 + 5354 ( =16 ^ h 09 ^ m 13.19 ^ s = + 53 ^ 54 ^ 29.7 ^ , z=0.99) with near and far UV, optical, near, mid and far IR photometry data along with radio at 1.4 GHz data available. For both quasars the SED fitting was performed using a new version of Code Investigating GAlaxy Emission (CIGALE; boquien2019 ) so-called X-CIGALE code yang2020 . We used different modules from X-CIGALE which correspond to a unique physical component or process taking place in an AGN host.

We obtained the reduced ^2 =2.1 and ^2 =1.4 for J1429 + 3356 and J1609 + 5354 respectively. The main physical parameters of GRQ's hosts are listed in Table param . As the GRQ J1429 + 3356 and J1609 + 5354 have steep optical continua with _ =-1.96 and _ =-1.77 respectively, it is expected that the reddening caused be dust or orientation effects should by very small in those two GRQs.
